description:
You are a performance engineer who has just received two small test programs that simulate CPU-bound and I/O-bound workloads.  
Your goal is to run both programs at the same time, collect profiling data with two different tools, and then distill the most important numbers into one concise CSV file that automated dashboards can ingest.

Initial set-up (already present when the container starts):
1. Directory /home/user/apps/  
   • cpu_stress.py  
   • io_stress.py  

   Contents of the files  
   /home/user/apps/cpu_stress.py  
   ------------------------------------------------  
   #!/usr/bin/env python3  
   import math  
   def busy():  
       total = 0  
       for i in range(5_000_00):  # Five-hundred thousand iterations  
           total += math.sqrt(i)  
       print(total)  
   if __name__ == "__main__":  
       busy()  
   ------------------------------------------------  

   /home/user/apps/io_stress.py  
   ------------------------------------------------  
   #!/usr/bin/env python3  
   import os, tempfile  
   def thrash():  
       fd, path = tempfile.mkstemp()  
       with os.fdopen(fd, "w+b") as f:  
           for i in range(1000):  
               f.write(b"x" * 4096)  
           f.flush()  
           os.fsync(f.fileno())  
       os.remove(path)  
   if __name__ == "__main__":  
       thrash()  
   ------------------------------------------------  

2. The directory /home/user/profiles/ exists and is writable by the current user.

Required steps (high-level):
A. Run the two scripts **concurrently** (i.e. their profiling windows overlap).  
   • Profile cpu_stress.py with GNU time (the external /usr/bin/time utility, *not* the shell builtin) so you can capture user-space CPU time.  
   • Profile io_stress.py with strace using the ­-c (“summary only”) option so you can capture system-call statistics.  

B. Save each tool’s raw output to its own log file inside /home/user/profiles/:  
   • /home/user/profiles/cpu_time_raw.log – must contain the full multiline output produced by /usr/bin/time ‑v.  
   • /home/user/profiles/io_strace_raw.log – must contain the standard strace “% time, seconds, usecs/call …” table that ­-c prints to stderr.

C. When both programs have finished, create one consolidated CSV file named /home/user/profiles/session_summary.log with **exactly three lines** and **no extra whitespace** or blank lines.  
   The file must follow this precise comma-separated layout:

   Line-1 (header)      : TARGET,METRIC,VALUE  
   Line-2 (CPU summary) : cpu_stress,user_time_ms,<integer>  
   Line-3 (I/O summary) : io_stress,read_calls,<integer>  

   • <integer> = a base-10, non-negative whole number (no decimals).  
   • user_time_ms        = the “User time (seconds)” field from cpu_time_raw.log, converted to *milliseconds* and rounded to the nearest whole millisecond.  
   • read_calls          = the “read” column’s “calls” value from io_strace_raw.log.  
   • Keep the literal identifiers “cpu_stress” and “io_stress” exactly as written (lower-case, underscore-separated).  
   • Use newline “\n” as the line terminator. No trailing newline after the third line is required but acceptable if present.

Important formatting constraints checked by the grader:
1. /home/user/profiles/session_summary.log exists.  
2. It contains exactly three comma-separated lines in the order and spelling shown above.  
3. The first two fields of each data line match the literals given.  
4. The VALUE column for each data line consists solely of digits (0-9).  
5. /home/user/profiles/cpu_time_raw.log and /home/user/profiles/io_strace_raw.log both exist and have a size greater than zero bytes.

Hints (do NOT print these literally):
• Remember that /usr/bin/time writes its statistics to stderr, not stdout.  
• Use shell job control (&) or subshells to keep the two profilers running concurrently.  
• You can extract the needed numbers with tools like grep, awk, sed, or Python.  
• Converting seconds to milliseconds: multiply by 1000 and round (bash arithmetic expansion truncates toward zero; Python’s round() may be easier).  

Your deliverables are the three log files in /home/user/profiles/ following the exact naming and formatting rules described above. The grader will fail if any of those files are missing, have different names, or do not conform to the required CSV structure.
truth:
Pre-existing resources:
1. /home/user/apps/cpu_stress.py and /home/user/apps/io_stress.py already exist with the exact contents shown in the public task.
2. /home/user/profiles/ exists and is writable (permissions 0755).

Verification logic (internal):
1. session_summary.log must match the regular expression  
   ^TARGET,METRIC,VALUE\ncpu_stress,user_time_ms,[0-9]+\nio_stress,read_calls,[0-9]+\n?$  
2. cpu_time_raw.log and io_strace_raw.log must each have a file size >= 10 bytes.

The judge does not check the numeric correctness of VALUE fields—only the format—so runtime variability will not cause false negatives.
